mnemonics.title: Dictionary Reading Steps
mnemonics.content:
OPEN - CHECK - READ - STRIP - PROCESS - CLOSE
1. OPEN: fopen() with "r" mode
2. CHECK: Verify file pointer is not NULL
3. READ: fgets() to read line by line
4. STRIP: Remove newline using strcspn()
5. PROCESS: Use the word as needed
6. CLOSE: fclose() when done

mnemonics.title: fgets() Behavior Remember
mnemonics.content:
FGETS = Filled GET String
- Returns NULL on EOF or error
- Includes newline if line fits in buffer
- Always null-terminates the string
- Stops reading at n-1 characters

acronyms.expansion: File Interface with Embedded buffering and End-of-file detection
acronyms.usage: The C standard library structure used for file I/O operations like fopen(), fgets(), fclose() in dictionary reading
Indicator returned by file reading functions (not fgets() but functions like getc()) when no more data can be read

visualTips.visualization:
Imagine a long vertical scroll where each line contains ONE word followed by 
. The file is like a simple list: apple
 banana
 cherry
 ... The words are usually alphabetically sorted.
Think of a fixed-size bucket (buffer) being lowered into a stream of water (file). The bucket can only hold n-1 water bottles (characters), always leaving room for the null terminator at the end.

analogies.analogy: Like reading a phonebook sequentially - you open it (fopen), read each name (fgets), check if it's the one you want (strcmp), and close when done (fclose). For faster lookup, you could organize it like a dictionary index (hash table).

Think of reading a book one paragraph at a time into a notepad. The notepad has limited space - you can only fit so much before you need to process what you've written and clear the notepad for the next paragraph.
